1: The priestly service rubrics begin; Leviticus 6 addresses what the priests do after the offering is presented. The ongoing priestly ministry — altar tending, fire maintenance, distribution of portions — is the type of Christ's ongoing high-priestly ministry after his one sacrifice: he 'always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25), maintaining the eternal covenant through perpetual intercession.
2: The asham for interpersonal fraud gains its cultic dimension from the false oath in YHWH's name. Christ tells Pilate he speaks truth (John 18:37); he speaks truth before the Sanhedrin that will condemn him (Mark 14:62). The truthfulness of the witness provides the foundation for the atonement that covers oath-breaking; Christ who never swore falsely covers those who have.
3: The false oath as the hinge that turns interpersonal tort into sacrilege. The profanation of the divine name through false swearing is the deepest category of speech violation. Christ's Name is the one invoked by believers in prayer, baptism, and confession; his perfect truthfulness ensures that the Name invoked is not an empty guarantor but the living reality behind every believing confession.
4: Restoration of the specific item taken — restitution precedes the sacrifice. The principle that relationship-repair must accompany sacrifice is stated in Christ's teaching: 'Leave your gift there before the altar and go. First be reconciled to your brother, and then come and offer your gift' (Matthew 5:23-24). The asham's mandatory restitution-before-sacrifice structure underlies the Lord's teaching on reconciliation as a prerequisite for worship.
5: The chomesh paid on the day of guilt-realization — same-day restitution, not delayed compliance. The urgency of restitution mirrors Christ's once-for-all atoning act; the cross is not a promissory note but a fully accomplished payment. 'It is finished' (John 19:30: tetelestai, the commercial term for 'paid in full') is the declaration that the restitution has been completed on the day required, without delay.
6: The ram guilt offering after full restitution — the sacrifice follows the civil restoration, not replacing it. Both are required. Christ's sacrifice provides the cultic atonement dimension; his active obedience provides the civil restitution dimension. The asham structure requires both: the cross removes guilt before God; the overflow of his righteousness restores what was owed to God's justice.
7: The comprehensive forgiveness formula: 'he shall be forgiven for any of the things that one may do and thereby become guilty.' The breadth of the formula covers every category of interpersonal wrong that has a perjury dimension. Christ's forgiveness is equally comprehensive: 'The blood of Jesus his Son cleanses us from all sin' (1 John 1:7) — not a category list but a universal coverage.
8: The altar fire that must never go out — the perpetual burnt offering maintained through the night and renewed each morning. The eternal altar fire is the type of Christ's eternal priesthood: 'he holds his priesthood permanently, because he continues forever... he always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:24-25). The never-quenched fire on the altar of the wilderness sanctuary was the anticipation of the permanently efficacious sacrifice of the one who 'entered once for all into the holy places' (Hebrews 9:12).
9: The olah on the hearth all night until morning. The burnt offering placed at evening burns through the darkness of the night. Christ's body lay in the tomb through the darkness of death — the darkness of Gethsemane, the three hours of darkness at Golgotha (Matthew 27:45), the sealed tomb — before the morning of resurrection. The altar fire through the night is the type of the divine life that persists through death's darkness.
10: The priestly linen garments for ash removal — even the mundane task of clearing ashes requires sacred vestments. The priestly ministry has no secular zone; even the removal of the remnants of yesterday's sacrifice is sacred work. Christ's priestly ministry is continuous: the 'ashes' of human failure — the accumulated waste of lives lived apart from God — are tended by the eternal Priest who makes 'all things new' (Revelation 21:5).
11: The change of garments for carrying ashes outside the camp — the two-stage holiness gradient. Christ 'suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood' (Hebrews 13:12). The ashes carried outside the camp to the clean place anticipate the sin-laden offering burned outside the camp (Leviticus 4:12,21; Leviticus 16:27) and ultimately the crucifixion outside Jerusalem's walls.
12: The daily wood added to the fire, the arrangement of the burnt offering, the burning of the peace offering fat — the ongoing priestly service as the pattern of Christ's continual intercession. The priest who kept the fire alive maintained the possibility of ongoing atonement; Christ 'always lives to make intercession' (Hebrews 7:25), maintaining the permanent opening to YHWH that his sacrifice achieved.
13: Esh tamid tuqad al-ha-mizbeach lo tikhbeh — fire shall be kept burning on the altar continually; it shall not go out. The eternal altar fire is the single most direct spatial type in the sanctuary of Christ's eternal priesthood. The fire that Leviticus 9:24 records as descending from YHWH at the sanctuary's inauguration must never be quenched. The priestly responsibility to maintain it is fulfilled by the one who is 'the same yesterday and today and forever' (Hebrews 13:8) — his priestly intercession knows no quenching.
14: The grain offering rubric — priestly handling after presentation. The grain offering is the offering of the fruit of human labor (field, mill, oven) given to YHWH. Christ receives the fruit of his people's lives — their deeds, prayers, service — as the High Priest who presents them before the Father: 'through him let us continually offer up a sacrifice of praise to God' (Hebrews 13:15). The ongoing grain offering ministry pictures the perpetual mediation of human worship through the priestly Christ.
15: The handful burned as azkarah before YHWH — the token that represents the whole. The azkarah (memorial/reminder) of the grain offering anticipates the Lord's Supper's 'do this in remembrance (anamnesis) of me' (Luke 22:19; 1 Corinthians 11:24-25). The Supper is not merely a memorial for the disciples but a presenting of Christ's sacrifice before the Father — an ongoing azkarah that the eternal High Priest makes of his one offering.
16: The priestly portion of the grain offering eaten unleavened in a holy place. The priests eat the holy bread within the sanctuary courts. Christ feeds his people with himself: 'I am the bread of life' (John 6:35,48,51). The priests' eating of the holy grain offering is the type of the eucharistic feast in which the people of God feed on the bread of Christ's body — the unleavened bread of sincerity and truth (1 Corinthians 5:8).
17: Most holy — the grain offering shares the highest sanctity category with chatat and asham. Christ's sacrifice encompasses all three: he is the chatat (sin offering that atones), the asham (guilt offering that restores), and the minchah (grain offering that consecrates the life to God). The three-fold most-holy category is united in one offering: 'For our sake he made him to be sin who knew no sin, so that in him we might become the righteousness of God' (2 Corinthians 5:21).
18: Whatever touches the most-holy grain offering shall become holy (kol asher yigga bahem yiqdash). The consecrating touch — contact with the most holy makes holy rather than defiling. This is the reverse of impurity transmission. Christ's touch cleanses and consecrates: touching the leper makes him clean (Mark 1:41-42), touching Jairus's dead daughter raises her (Mark 5:41), the woman touching his garment is healed (Mark 5:27-29). The consecrating principle of the most-holy offering is embodied in every healing touch of the incarnate High Priest.
19: The ordination grain offering introduced — the minchat chinuch for Aaron and his sons. The ordination of Aaron foreshadows the ordination of the eternal High Priest. While Aaron's ordination involves washing, clothing, and anointing (Lev 8), Christ's priestly ordination involves baptism (the Father's voice: 'This is my beloved Son,' Matthew 3:17), transfiguration (the confirmation of glory, Matthew 17:5), and resurrection (the definitive appointment: 'declared to be the Son of God in power... by his resurrection from the dead,' Romans 1:4).
20: The high priest's daily grain offering divided equally between morning and evening. Christ is the morning and evening offering: 'He himself bore our sins in his body on the tree... by his wounds you have been healed' (1 Peter 2:24). The entire day — from the morning of his resurrection ministry to the evening of his intercession — is covered by the continuous priestly grain offering of his life given in service to the Father and intercession for his people.
21: Prepared on the griddle with oil — the minchah chavitin's preparation. The oil mixed through the grain offering is the type of the Spirit's presence in Christ's priestly ministry: 'God anointed Jesus of Nazareth with the Holy Spirit and with power' (Acts 10:38). The Spirit-soaked offering is the type of the Spirit-anointed Priest whose every action is empowered by the eternal Spirit (Hebrews 9:14: 'who through the eternal Spirit offered himself without blemish to God').
22: Kalil le-YHWH tiqtar — it shall be wholly burned to YHWH. The high priest's grain offering is entirely consumed on the altar; no portion is retained by the priest for himself. Christ's self-offering is total: 'he offered up himself' (Hebrews 7:27); 'the Son of Man came not to be served but to serve, and to give his life as a ransom for many' (Mark 10:45). Nothing of the offering is held back; the priestly self-donation is complete.
23: Every grain offering of a priest shall be wholly burned; it shall not be eaten. The priest cannot eat his own offering: he cannot be simultaneously the mediator of atonement and the recipient of its benefits in the same transaction. Yet Christ resolves this paradox: as both priest and sacrifice, he offers himself fully; and as the risen Lord, he shares the resurrection-life that his death purchased — he does not eat his own sin-offering, but he does feast with his redeemed people at the eschatological table (Revelation 19:9; Matthew 26:29).
24: The sin offering rubric — where the burnt offering is killed, the sin offering is killed. The spatial unification of the most-solemn offerings at the same altar location confirms that all sacrifices form a single system converging on a single point: the altar as the place of substitution. The cross is the one altar on which all the types converge — the sin offering, the burnt offering, the guilt offering all find their single fulfillment at Golgotha.
25: The sin offering is most holy and must be slaughtered in the northern (most holy) altar location. The dignity of the sin offering — carrying the guilt of others — is the highest, not the lowest, category of sacrifice. Christ's cross is not his humiliation alone but his glorification: 'Now is the Son of Man glorified, and God is glorified in him' (John 13:31). The cross is the throne (John 12:32), the altar (Hebrews 13:10), and the holy place simultaneously.
26: The priest who offers the sin offering shall eat it — priestly identification with the sin borne. The priest who performs the atonement eats the offering, literally ingesting the sin-laden sacrifice. Christ's 'eating' of our sin is described in 2 Corinthians 5:21: 'he made him to be sin who knew no sin.' The priestly consumption of the sin offering is a type of Christ's total taking-on of human sin — not merely handling it but bearing it in his own person.
27: Sin offering blood on a garment must be laundered in the holy place. The blood's holiness-contamination effect on fabric — purifying rather than defiling — points to Revelation 7:14: the great multitude 'have washed their robes and made them white in the blood of the Lamb.' The laundering-in-holy-place requirement becomes the laundering-in-Christ's-blood through which the redeemed are clothed in white (Revelation 3:5; Leviticus 7:9).
28: The earthen vessel used for sin offering meat must be broken; the bronze vessel must be scoured. Earthenware absorbs permanently and cannot be recleaned; it must be destroyed. The principle of permanent absorption — that sin-laden sacrifice leaves an indelible mark on its container — anticipates the truth that Christ permanently bore the mark of his sin-bearing: the risen Christ retains the wounds (John 20:27). The nail-prints and the pierced side are the permanent 'absorption' marks of the sin offering that cannot be effaced even by resurrection.
29: Every male among the priests may eat the sin offering — most holy access is for the priestly family. In Christ, all believers are made 'a royal priesthood' (1 Peter 2:9) with access to the 'most holy' of Christ's sacrifice: 'we have confidence to enter the holy places by the blood of Jesus' (Hebrews 10:19). The restriction to male priests in the Levitical type gives way to the universal priestly access of the new covenant community.
30: The sin offering whose blood was brought inside the tent of meeting is burned up, not eaten. The most intense atonement acts — blood manipulation in the most holy place — require burning outside the camp rather than priestly eating. 'Jesus also suffered outside the gate in order to sanctify the people through his own blood. Therefore let us go to him outside the camp' (Hebrews 13:11-13). The outside-camp burning of the most-holy sin offering is the direct type of the crucifixion outside Jerusalem.
